content.text:
Analyze @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md only focus on Tones section, the description about each tone, and the "apply rules". Review them to find any gaps, contradictions...

Don't change any content. Incomplete example is acceptable.

Discuss with me to improve the specification.

content.thinking: I need to look at the specification file to understand what I'm working with.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character has many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have seperated section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the process is reset (usually when starting new word).
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word has many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Scan pseudo-word
139: 
140: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
141:   the tone in the correct position.
142: - The existing characters must not be empty.
143: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
144: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
145:   character to scan, then stop.
146: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
147:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
148: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
149:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
150:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
151: 
152: #### Tone position rules
153: 
154: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
155: 
156: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
157:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
158: - Exceptions:
159:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
160: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
161: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
162:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
163:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
164:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
165:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
166:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
167:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
168:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
169:     consonants, put on second vowel.
170:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
171: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
172: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking: I'm going through the Tones section to identify any gaps or contradictions in how they're defined.

content.text:
Here is my review of the Tones section. I focused on the descriptions of each tone, the **Apply rules**, **Scan pseudo-word**, and **Tone position rules**.

## Gaps

### 1. No tone-replacement / re-tone behavior
The spec says "all of them don't have any tone (level), use tone position rules". This implies if any vowel already has a tone, nothing happens. But Telex normally allows replacing an existing tone, e.g. `Aù` + `s` → `Á`. There's no rule for:
- Replacing a non-level tone with another tone (e.g., `Ás` → `?`).
- Whether `Z`/`z` for "reset to level" is also subject to **Apply rules** (currently `Z` is not in the trigger list at line 130).

### 2. `Z`/`z` (level reset) is undefined operationally
Line 94 says use `Z`/`z` to reset, but:
- `Z` is missing from the trigger list in **Apply rules** (line 130 only lists `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J`).
- No mention of where `Z` finds the toned vowel (does it reuse Scan pseudo-word? Tone position rules?).
- No mention of what happens when `Z` is typed and there is no existing tone (literal? no-op?).

### 3. "Cancel" / undo a just-typed tone
Telex commonly allows typing the same tone trigger again to remove it (e.g., `As` → `Á`, then `s` → `As`). This is not addressed.

### 4. Literal fallback on failure isn't explicit about what "literal" means
Lines 129, 134, 136 say "append the new character literally", but it's unclear:
- Does the input switch into literal mode (like the diacritic Cancel rules at lines 67–71), or is just *that one* keystroke literal?
- If it's just that one keystroke, can the next `s` still trigger a tone? This needs to be stated to match or contrast with the diacritic behavior.

### 5. Case of the trigger character itself
The diacritic section (line 45) explicitly preserves the existing character's case. The tone section never specifies what happens to the case of the *trigger* `S/F/R/X/J` — presumably consumed entirely, but it should be stated. Also, does an uppercase `S` vs lowercase `s` affect anything?

### 6. Consonant definition is implicit
**Scan pseudo-word** uses "consonant (singular)" but Vietnamese has digraphs/trigraphs (`ch`, `gh`, `kh`, `nh`, `ph`, `th`, `tr`, `ngh`, `ng`, `gi`, `qu`). The word "singular" hints at single character, but:
- "Singular" is not defined.
- Then later, line 165–166 treats `GI` and `QU` as compound consonants. Contradiction with the singular-consonant scanning model.

### 7. Final-consonant cluster `nh`, `ng`, `ch` not addressed
Iterating "backward until scanned the first consonant (singular)" would stop at `h` in `ánh`, leaving the `n` outside. The pseudo-word window then becomes wrong, but tone placement still works because tone is on the vowel — yet the spec promises a structural shape `<vowels><consonants>` that won't hold. Need to clarify if final clusters are scanned as one unit or whether stopping at the first one is intentional and harmless.

### 8. Behavior at word boundary / what resets the buffer
The Apply rules reference "the buffer" and "existing characters" but never define when the buffer starts/ends. The diacritic Cancel rule mentions "starting new word" (line 69) but Tones never reference it. A separator (space, punctuation, Enter) presumably resets but should be stated.

### 9. `Y` standalone words
`Y` is a vowel (line 87). What about a word that is just `Y` (e.g., `Ý`, `Ỷ`)? Scan pseudo-word produces `<vowels>` of just `Y`. Single vowel case applies. This works, but worth a confirming example since it's an edge case.

### 10. Unicode normalization
Not stated whether the result is NFC or NFD. Affects `Ế` (Ê + acute) which can be one or two codepoints. This is a gap that affects implementation correctness and matching the existing diacritic state (e.g., when a vowel is already toned via paste).

## Contradictions / Ambiguities

### A. "all of them don't have any tone (level)" vs implicit re-tone
Line 134 explicitly forbids applying when any vowel already has a tone. This contradicts how Telex actually works and contradicts (or at least omits) the `Z`-reset semantics: how can you reset to level via `Z` if the apply step refuses to run on already-toned vowels?

### B. `<consonant>` vs `<consonants>` in Scan pseudo-word output
Line 148–150:
- "until scanned the first consonant (singular)" suggests a *single* consonant.
- But the resulting shapes use `<consonants>` (plural) at the start of `<vowels><consonants>` and `<consonant><vowels><consonants>` (singular at the front, plural at the back).
- Inconsistent: leading consonant scanning seems to stop at one char, but trailing consonant cluster is plural. Is the back scan greedy and front scan singular? The rule text says "first consonant (singular)" for both directions.

### C. `QU` exception is doubly stated
Line 159 says `QU` alone "could not put tone, this is a consonant only".
Line 165–166 says words starting with `QU` (with more vowels) put tone on the next vowel.
These don't directly contradict, but the first bullet is somewhat redundant — if `QU` alone has no `<vowels>`, why is it a special "Exceptions" section? Worth either justifying (because `U` would otherwise be classified as a vowel) or removing.

### D. `OO` in tone position rules
Line 167–169 lists `OO` (e.g., `xoong`, `coong`) as a special pair. But `OO` as two identical vowels would also collide with the Diacritic **Apply rules** (line 39 `Oo` → `Ô`). So the user can never input the literal `OO` unless they bypass diacritics. This makes `OO` in tone rules unreachable. Either:
- Confirm `OO` rule is for legacy paste/imported content, or
- The interaction needs to be documented.

### E. `Ơ` priority vs `Ư` priority
Line 162 puts `Ơ` first.
Line 163 puts `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` next, with priority left-to-right.
What about `ƯƠ` (e.g., `ướp`)? Both contain marks. Rule says `Ơ` wins → `ướp` (correct). Good.
But `ƯA` (e.g., `mưa` toned as `múa`/`mùa`) — only `Ư` is in the marked list, so it goes on `Ư`? Real Vietnamese: `mùa` puts tone on `u` (Ư with no horn after pronunciation, but here Ư+a). Result is `mùa` — tone on `u`. Spec says tone on `Ư`. Consistent. Worth verifying with `ia`, `ua`, `ưa` though — these are diphthongs where the *first* vowel is stressed; the spec only handles them via `Ư` in the marked list, not `i`/`u`. So `mua`+`f` → `mùa`? Rule "Otherwise, put on first vowel" → `ù` on first → `mùa`. Correct. But `tia`+`s` → `tía`? "Otherwise, first vowel" → tone on `i`? Real: `tía` (tone on `i`). Spec produces `tịa` style on first → on `t`'s next vowel `i` → `tía`. Correct. Just confirm with examples.

### F. New "modern" tone style (last vowel rule)
Vietnamese has two competing tone-placement conventions: classic (`hóa`) and modern (`hoá`). Spec implements classic only. Should explicitly state this is intentional and not configurable.

### G. Diacritic ordering vs tone trigger ordering
What about `Awf` vs `Afw`? The first does `A`→`Ă`, then `f`→`Ằ`. The second: `A`+`f`→`À`, then `w` — does the diacritic Apply rule (line 36) "Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty)" block this? `À` has tone but no character diacritic; so `w` should still apply Breve, giving `Ằ`. Both orderings should yield `Ằ`. Worth an explicit example showing tones and character diacritics interleave correctly, especially since "diacritic" in line 36 might be misread as including tones.

## Summary of items to discuss

1. Add tone-replacement semantics (existing tone overridden by new trigger).
2. Specify `Z`/`z` reset behavior fully (trigger list, scan, position, no-op cases).
3. Add cancel/undo: same trigger removes the tone.
4. Define what "append literally" means: one-shot literal, or switch-to-literal-mode like diacritic Cancel rules.
5. Clarify case-handling of trigger characters and whether `S`/`s` differ.
6. Define "consonant (singular)" vs digraph/trigraph handling in scanning.
7. Make front-vs-back scan greediness consistent (`<consonant>` vs `<consonants>`).
8. Define buffer reset boundary for tones (matches diacritic spec).
9. Reconcile or remove `OO` from tone position rules given diacritic conflict.
10. State clearly: classic tone-placement only; document `ƯA`/`UA`/`IA` outcomes with examples.
11. Clarify "diacritic" in the Apply rule (line 36) excludes tone marks, and confirm tones+diacritics commute.
12. State Unicode normalization (NFC vs NFD).

Which of these would you like to address first?
